title-group.article-title: Breeding practice of indigenous village chickens, and traits and breed preferences of smallholder farmers

article-meta.aff: 2 Present address: Department of Biology, College of Science and Mathematics Education Kotebe University of Education Addis Ababa Ethiopia
3 Present address: College of Agriculture and Environmental Science Arsi University Asela Ethiopia

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceTakele Taye Desta, Wolaita Sodo Agricultural, Technical, Vocational, Education and Training College, Wolaita Sodo, Ethiopia. Email: takele_taye@yahoo.com

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Indigenous village chickens, or (IVCs), significantly contribute to rural livelihoods. Although natural selection has a disproportionate impact on the genetic structure of IVCs, farmers have developed locally tailored breeding practices to get the most out of their flocks.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: Small‐scale farmers’ insights on trait breed preferences and family flock breeding practices are presented in this cross‐sectional study.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A cross‐sectional study was conducted in two agroecological zones using face‐to‐face individual interviews with 119 general informants.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Farmers prefer IVCs because they are multipurpose birds. Unlike policymakers, who usually underestimate the importance of IVCs, small‐scale farmers acknowledge the coexistence of local and commercial chickens. Only 15.7% of farmers recruited homegrown cocks, whereas 47.9% outsourced breeding cocks from local markets and 36.4% from neighbours (χ‐squared = 15.976, df = 2, p = 0.0003395). About 49.2% of small‐scale farmers believed that consanguineous mating‐induced inbreeding has only trivial effects. High flock turnover significantly reduces inbreeding. Regardless of the low production capacity, small‐scale farmers prefer local (rank = 1.47) chickens to commercial (rank = 1.61). For cocks, fertility and growth traits were highly sought after, whereas for hens, maternal instincts and laying performance were prioritized. Compared to the highlands, the lowlands had a longer egg storage period (t = 2.677, df = 117, p = 0.009, 95% CI: −3.7607, −0.5622).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This study documented the wisdom of small‐scale farmers and encouraged the incorporation of their insights into a sustainable genetic improvement program.

abstract.p: Graphical Abstract: Local chickens significantly contribute to the rural livelihood of the less developed world. The trait and breed preference of small‐scale farmers and their nonconventional breeding practices are primarily driven by the adaptive characteristics of local chickens and the demographic structure of the small‐scale farmers. A sustainable breeding plan that addresses the preference of small‐scale farmers and matches the production environment needs to consider the heterogeneity of small‐scale farmers’ breeding practices and their practical application in rural settings.

p.mixed-citation: Desta, T. T., & Wakeyo, O. (2024). Breeding practice of indigenous village chickens, and traits and breed preferences of smallholder farmers. Veterinary Medicine and Science, 10, e1517. 10.1002/vms3.151738952253

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Natural selection, which is the primary force in evolution and the source of genetic variation, shapes the genetic structure of outbred indigenous village chickens (IVCs). According to Bernatchez and Landry (2003), natural selection favours polymorphism, resulting in resilient populations. Footprints of selection are left behind by both strong natural selection and mild artificial selection. IVCs are subject to varying degrees of selection pressure. IVCs are important model animals for studying the combined effects of strong natural selection and weak anthropogenic effects as well as their individual effects. The high genetic diversity of IVCs (Desta, 2015; Lyimo et al., 2014) is characterized by a wide variety of phenogenetic characteristics (genetic variants and underlying traits) (Desta et al., 2013). As a result of this high diversity, which is vital for evolutionary processes to function properly, both natural and artificial selection have behaved in a relaxed mode.

Artificial selection has been limited to a small extent in IVCs. Particularly, small‐scale farmers’ broad breeding objectives (Bett et al., 2011) and the continuous exposure of IVCs to natural selection have lessened the impact of human‐induced selection. Due to human‐induced weak selection pressure and changes in production systems, IVCs greatly differ from their wild cousins and presumed ancestors, the jungle fowls (Desta, 2019, 2021b). Because of their intermediate performance (Desta, 2021b) and genetic structure (Desta, 2015), IVCs can connect production breeds that have been significantly impacted by human‐driven selection pressure and conventional management systems with junglefowl that are practically surviving under the decisive impact of natural selection.

Small‐scale farmers have significantly varied preferences when it comes to traits that are important to their sociocultural and economic needs. However, they share most of the preferences associated with resilience traits. IVCs provide various products and services due to their unique characteristics. Both consumers and subsistence farmers show a high demand for meat and eggs with better flavour and a variety of morphological characteristics possessed by IVCs (FAO, 2010; Yakubu et al., 2020). The pigmentation of the meat also influences preference (Thieme et al., 2014).

Breeds and trait preferences of small‐scale farmers are influenced by the demands of the community, the nature of the production system and their interactions. It is important to acquire knowledge of the trait and breed preferences of small‐scale farmers to improve the performance of the IVC production system in a sustainable as well as environmentally friendly way. To develop an eco‐friendly breeding program, community‐based breeding systems need to incorporate indigenous knowledge and expertise. This study reports the breeds and traits preferences of small‐scale farmers and their breeding practices.

sec.title: The study sites
sec.p: The study site, the Wolaita zone, is found in southern Ethiopia between 6.4° and 7.1° north latitude and 37.4° and 38.2° east longitude. The elevation of Wolaita ranges from 1200 to 2950 meters above sea level (masl). Based on elevation and according to the customary classification method of agro‐ecological zones adopted in Ethiopia, Wolaita is divided into three agro‐ecological zones: kolla or lowland (35%, less than 1500 masl), woina dega or intermediate highland (56%, 1500–2400 masl) and dega or highland (9%, greater than 2400 masl). The main rainy season typically lasts from July to October, while the minor rainy season usually lasts from March to May. The average daily temperature is 19.9°C. The average daily temperature in July is 17.7°C and in February and March, it is 22.1°C. The average annual precipitation is 1014 mm.

sec.title: Sampling methods
sec.p: A cross‐sectional study was conducted in two districts of the Wolaita zone; Humbo, which represents the agroecology of the lowlands, and Damot Gale, which represents the highlands. In consultation with the livestock extension advisory service of the sampled districts, the study was carried out in six representative rural villages (a.k.a. kebeles) chosen from the two agroecological zones (i.e. three from each agroecological zone). A master list of farmers in each village was used to select the farmers who were interviewed from each village. Consequently, the first farmer was selected at random from the first‐class interval using a lottery method after the total number of farmers on the list was divided by 20 (village sample size). The remaining 19 farmers were chosen at a fixed interval that was proportional to the class interval's size. After receiving verbal consent from 20 farmers in each village (apart from Taba, where only 19 farmers were interviewed), a trained enumerator administered a semi‐structured questionnaire to 119 farmers. The data were gathered through a one‐on‐one face‐to‐face interview.

sec.title: The studied traits
sec.p: Most of the questions asked of farmers were about their demographics, breeding methods, selection of hens and cocks, and eggs, and preferred types of breeds and traits. Explanatory variables typically included the respondents’ gender, education level and agroecological zone. Weighted ranks were used to prioritize farmers’ preferences for the stated breeds and traits.

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: IBM SPSS Statistics 23 (IBM Corp., 2015) and R (R Core Team, 2016) were used to conduct a statistical analysis. The data were analysed using the Chi‐square test and the t test. SPSS was used to create summary statistics; R was used to produce Chi‐square test results; and Excel was used to generate graphical presentations.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: The focus of this study is primarily on the traits and breeds that small‐scale farmers prefer, the origin of the breeding flock, the effects of consanguineous mating and the desired characteristics of IVCs and eggs that are intended to hatch.

sec.title: The characteristics of a good quality hen
sec.p: Table 1 displays the frequency counts of the attributes of a good‐quality hen. According to the weighted rank, farmers had the highest preference for economically important sex‐limited traits.

caption.p: Preference ranking is made by farmers while selecting hens for different attributes (1 is the most preferred trait, whereas 8 is the least preferred 1).

sec.title: Selection criteria for a breeding cock
sec.p: Farmers were asked to rank five traits that have been known to demonstrate the good qualities of a cock. The respondents placed much emphasis on fertility and growth traits (Table 2). This choice has considerable overlap with the preferred traits of hens.

caption.p: Preference ranking is made by farmers while selecting cocks for different attributes (rank 1 represents the most preferred trait, whereas rank 5 is the least preferred 1).

sec.title: Source of a breeding cock
sec.p: Most respondents (47.9%) reported that they got their breeding cocks from the local market, whereas neighbours’ flocks only made‐up 36.4%, and homegrown breeding cocks made up the smallest proportion (15.7%). There are statistically significant differences in the proportions of the reported sources of breeding cocks (χ‐squared = 15.976, df = 2 and p = 0.0003395). Indeed, cocks come from all over the marketshed to weekly dedicated markets. The flock turnover that offsets the negative effects of inbreeding can be realized in this acquisition pattern of cocks. However, purchasing chickens from the open market might speed up the spread of contagions.

sec.title: Storage of eggs
sec.p: There are statistically significant differences between the highland and lowland regions in the number of days the eggs were stored before being incubated (t = 2.677, df = 117, p = 0.009 and 95% CI: −3.7607, −0.5622). Table 3 shows the statistics for each group. With a mean and standard deviation of 17.403 ± 4.5180, a median of 16 and a mode of 15, the number of days the eggs were stored before incubation ranged from 10 to 30.

caption.p: Summary statistics of the length of days eggs stored before incubation and the proportion of eggs incubated from those laid in a clutch when the intention is hatching of chicks.

tr.td: The number of days the eggs stored before incubation
The proportion of incubated out‐of‐laid eggs in a clutch

sec.p: Most of the clutch's eggs were incubated. For example, Table 3 provides a summary of the proportion of incubated eggs from the total number of eggs laid in each clutch. There is a statistically significant difference between the highland and lowland regions in the proportion of eggs incubated out of the eggs laid in a clutch (t = 3.296, df = 114, p = 0.001 and 95% CI: 0.360, 1.444). Between 50% and 100% of a clutch's eggs were incubated, with a mean and standard deviation of 90.16% ± 15.36% and a median and a mode of 100%. When the breeding goal was to raise chicks, the median and mode values indicate that almost all the eggs laid in a clutch were incubated.

sec.title: Selection of egg for incubation
sec.p: A small percentage of respondents (29.2%) incubated selected eggs, indicating that most respondents did not practice the selection of eggs (χ‐squared = 16.26, df = 1 and p = 5.521e−05). The criteria shown in Figure 1 were used by farmers to select eggs, and the size and cleanliness of the eggs were highly considered.

caption.p: Selection criteria of eggs that are used for incubation.

sec.title: Impact of consanguineous mating
sec.p: Table 4 provides a summary of farmers’ responses to the impact of consanguineous mating, with statistically significant differences in the proportions reported (χ‐squared = 80.912, df = 4 and p = 2.2e−16). In general, there are two schools of thought regarding the reported effects of consanguineous mating: consanguineous mating significantly reduces the genetic vigour of the family flock, and it does not pose a significant threat to the flock's performance. Farmers keep cocks that are thought to be good for a long time because of their size, preferred plumage colour and robustness. By preserving desirable traits, farmers keep these cocks to enhance their flock's performance and economic worth. Uncontrolled breeding and a high cock‐to‐hen ratio, on the other hand, can significantly offset inbreeding. In addition, frequent sales, losses attributed to predators, disease, theft and occasional in‐house slaughter (particularly of cocks) for family consumption (especially during holidays) and to welcome guests of honour contribute to a high flock turnover.

caption.p: The impact of mating among closely related birds on the performance and genetic vigour of a family flock (multiple responses exist).

tr.td: Inbreeding does not impose a significant impact
Inbreeding aggravates the death of chicks
Inbreeding reduces the size of eggs laid

sec.title: The skill and wisdom of farmers in identifying the sex of baby chicks
sec.p: The baby chicks of small‐scale farmers are rarely identified by sex. Small‐scale farmers, on the other hand, have learned to identify the sex of baby chicks attributed to their long experience (Table 5).

caption.p: Traditional methods of identifying the sex of baby chicks.

tr.td: The growth of the tail feather

sec.title: Breed preference
sec.p: From three broad categories of chicken types – local, commercial and their genetic crosses – farmers were asked to rank their preferred breed. Local chickens received the highest preference (rank = 1.47), followed by production breeds (rank = 1.61) and crossbreds received the lowest preference (rank = 2.92). Figure 2 provides a summary of the reported reasons for preferring breeds. Farmers were very interested in keeping both commercial and local chickens so that they could use the best qualities of both types. This is because keeping local or commercial chickens would not entirely fulfil farmers’ preferences. For instance, local chickens excel in some of the stated preferences, whereas commercial chickens surpass them in others.

caption.p: Criteria that are used by small‐scale farmers to rank chicken types.

sec.title: Revealed preferences for local chickens
sec.p: Figure 3 provides a summary of the reported reasons for the high preference for local chickens. A long list of desirable characteristics of local chickens was mentioned by farmers; however, self‐reliance and resilience were emphasized. Local chickens’ genetic robustness and adaptive significance in low‐input, low‐output production systems are largely reflected in the desirable traits that have been reported.

caption.p: Reported reasons for high preference for local chickens (multiple responses exist).

sec.title: The undesirable qualities of local chickens
sec.p: Despite their genetic capacity for resilience and self‐reliance traits, local chickens have undesirable characteristics. Figure 4 shows the undesirable characteristics of local chickens. The respondents identified a couple of undesirable characteristics of local chickens that should be improved without significantly impacting their desirable attributes.

caption.p: The reported undesirable attributes of local chickens.

sec.title: The comparative advantage of keeping commercial chickens
sec.p: Figure 5 provides a summary of the production breeds’ desired characteristics. Due to their distinct breeding and management histories, the list of desirable characteristics of production breeds matches the undesirable features of local chickens. However, the traits of resilience and self‐reliance that are typical of local chickens are largely unfavourably correlated with these attributes in which production breeds excel. Therefore, before the massive genetic improvement of local chickens to produce more eggs and/or meat, trade‐offs ought to be evaluated.

caption.p: The desirable qualities of production breeds.

sec.title: The undesirable attributes of commercial chickens
sec.p: Figure 6 provides a summary of the undesirable characteristics of production breeds. To meet growing demand and maintain the profitability of vertically integrated large‐scale poultry farms, undesirable traits, that is, in the context of the small‐scale farmers’ production system, have been accumulated through decades of strong human‐driven selection for egg and/or meat production. Moreover, the base population's genetic robustness has been significantly diminished by heavy selection for production traits. Strong reverse selection or extensive cross‐breeding of production breeds with either local chickens or junglefowl is required to recover the lost allelic variants with adaptive importance.

caption.p: The reported undesirable qualities of production breeds.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Small‐scale farmers who keep IVCs do not have a single, dependable way to meet their diverse needs and preferences. Chickens are more than just a source of protein and companion animals for small‐scale farmers; additionally, they fulfil various cultural and ritual commitments and provide a variety of socioeconomic services (Desta, 2021a; Kapella et al., 2023; Manyelo et al., 2020; Wong et al., 2017). The use of chickens in the everyday lives of small‐scale farmers demonstrates their long tradition of breeding and managing chickens (Zhang et al., 2020). IVCs are somewhat deliberately selected by small‐scale farmers to fulfil their broad breeding objectives and adapt to the local environment. A significant proportion of the farmers who were sampled have not been aware of the negative impacts of inbreeding. However, Ethiopian chickens are unique in that they exhibit some genetic sub‐structuring (Desta, 2015), which is either virtually absent or found to be weak in other countries’ IVCs (e.g. Lyimo et al., 2014; Muchadeyi et al., 2007; Osei‐Amponsah et al., 2010). The phenogenetic structure of IVCs is significantly influenced by evolutionary forces and natural selection because of their unlimited interaction with the environment (Lafuente & Beldade, 2019). Genetic drift, which is caused by the small size of the family flock, is one of the evolutionary forces that shape IVCs’ genetic structure. However, a high level of (intricate web) gene flow, high turnover of the family flock, uncontrolled breeding and frequent intermingling of scavenging birds can significantly mitigate this effect.

As a result of these anthropogenic effects and evolutionary forces, IVCs have a distinct phenogenetic landscape and a high genetic diversity. Due to the broad genetic base of IVCs, farmers are compelled to select desired traits that are vital for local adaptation. High genetic diversity in IVCs is due to their breeding and management history (Bettridge et al., 2018) as well as the rich biodiversity of the tropical region (Perfecto & Vandermeer, 2008). IVCs are resilient and explorative (self‐reliant) because they are typically left to scavenge and express their instincts and possess innately trained phenotype. IVCs acquire a variety of desirable characteristics because of these management practices. These characteristics serve the needs of consumers, who naturally prefer IVC products and services, and are crucial to the livelihoods of small‐scale farmers.

However, IVCs only lay a few eggs (Mujyambere et al., 2021; Wong et al., 2017) due to the provision of various products and services, the allocation of resources to traits of resilience and self‐reliance and the absence of strong human‐driven selection (Desta, 2021a); they grow slowly (FAO, 2014) and produce fewer carcasses. In less developed countries where poor family planning is practiced due to cultural taboos and a lack of health care services (World Health Organization, 2020), IVCs are unable to feed the ever‐increasing human population. As a result, there is widespread malnutrition, which is especially detrimental for children, women who are pregnant, and the elderly. The health of the public as well as the income of small‐scale farmers are negatively impacted by the low production of eggs and meat. Policymakers and international donors intend to replace a significant proportion of IVCs with high‐yielding production breeds as a response to this low production (Seré, 2020). On the other hand, production breeds that have been heavily selected to produce more meat or eggs have lost a significant level of genetic robustness that would have allowed them to adapt to production systems with low inputs and outputs. This endeavour typically fails to achieve the intended objectives because it is based on incorrect assumptions. Genetic erosion has typically ruined the genetic robustness of IVCs (Dessie et al., 2011).

This conflicting interest could be resolved by putting into action a variety of breeding programs that are meticulously aligned with the local context. Production breeds, for instance, can be reared in urban and suburban areas with limited land. However, even urban dwellers favour IVC products attributable of their flavour and taste. In addition, when urban communities are permitted to perform ritual obligations, IVCs are the only type of chicken that can satisfy these requirements. By significantly improving the management conditions they require to express their genetic potential, production breeds can be introduced into rural areas with reliable markets. However, in this context, farmers ought to consider raising chickens as their primary means of subsistence. Experience shows that collecting production breeds from livestock extension advisory services or hatching units does little to improve management for small‐scale farmers who view chicken raising as a sideline activity. With the high level of care that production breeds require, suboptimal management of small‐scale farmers could not keep up.

A semi‐intensive management strategy may also be preferable for dual‐purpose breeds and even crosses. Farmers, on the other hand, show less preference for crosses because there is a possibility that these genetic groups do not match the extremes of farmers’ preferences – high production vs. hardiness. Improved IVC management practices may be sufficient in remote areas where there is low market demand for chicken products (FAO, 2004, 2014). Despite this, farmers are aware of the IVC's benefits and drawbacks (Bett et al., 2011) and production breeds, which may assist in the development of breeding programmes that are tailored to specific regions. Even though farmers have demonstrated a preference for both IVCs and production breeds on a different level, it is difficult to match farmers’ preferences with the extensive production system that is constrained by resources like feeds, management practices and veterinary services.

To outsource high‐quality birds and keep them in their flock, farmers have developed a variety of selection criteria. Adaptive traits dominate the selection criteria, and these birds need to offer a variety of products and services (Bett et al., 2011; Muchadeyi et al., 2009). However, unfavourable genetic and phenotypic correlations exist between production and resilience traits, which impede the desired genetic gain. However, the environment has a significant impact on most resilience traits, which have low heritability. As a result, improving the production environment can significantly offset the loss associated with hardiness‐inducing allelic variants. According to Nyamushamba et al. (2017), numerous attempts to enhance the local chicken genetic resource's productivity and production have frequently failed by disregarding farmers’ direct involvement in interventions and adopting results from controlled experiments carried out on extensive systems. According to Tham‐Agyekum et al. (2010), farmers typically do not keep written records and conduct conventional research but rather rely on recall. They have, however, mastered the agricultural system's resource flow through life experience.

The respondents have developed criteria for identifying the sex of baby chicks, and these criteria are significantly linked to secondary sexual characteristics (Estevez et al., 2002; Oleforuh‐Okoleh, 2011). However, these characteristics are easily discernible at sexual maturity, which occurs at ∼10 weeks (Oleforuh‐Okoleh, 2011), which raises some questions regarding the validity of the farmers’ sex identification methods.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: Smallholder farmers’ traits and breed preferences are discussed in this study. However, smallholder farmers sought after both production and resilience traits, which could not be met by keeping either indigenous or production breeds. This preference calls for the wise use of both indigenous and production breeds. Smallholder farmers have context‐based and novel breeding practices to make the best use of their flocks. The findings of this study are important inputs for creating and implementing long‐term genetic improvement programmes for the chicken production systems of small‐scale farmers. However, to further enrich the current state of knowledge, extensive reviews of research outputs and work on a diverse range of production environments are required due to the heterogeneity of the extensive production systems.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Takele Taye Desta and Oli Wakeyo were involved in data collection. Takele Taye Desta analysed the data and drafted the manuscript. Oli Wakeyo commented on the draft paper.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: FUNDING INFORMATION
sec.p: Not available.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: All participation in this study was entirely voluntary, and the data gathered from participants will only be used for research. Informed verbal consent was obtained from all the respondents.

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.1517.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
ack.p: The respondents’ original ideas are duly acknowledged.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: Data will be available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
